metadata.split_role: Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. Updated in v4.2.82 with 25-record mushroom credibility pass including Black Velvet Bolete and Blue-staining Slippery Jack. v4.2.83: mushroom bolete photo/credibility batch; Commons images added for Neoporphyrellus alboater and Suillus tomentosus; 25-record Suillus/Leccinum/Neoporphyrellus pass. v4.2.85 refreshed Blue-staining Slippery Jack image URLs to direct upload.wikimedia.org files and carried forward v4.2.83 image coverage. v4.2.86: optional photo patches are treated as fill-only for existing base-record image fields; direct image fallback hardened for Black Velvet Bolete and Suillus tomentosus where applicable. v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4: 25-record Suillus/Leccinum credibility pass; source/species-note issues reduced while image/local/taxonomy reviews were retained. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.culinary_uses: Edible by many field guides when correctly identified and cooked, but slimy and often modest quality. Many people peel the cap skin and discard old or buggy specimens.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.notes: In this guide, Slippery Jack means Suillus luteus: the slimy brown-capped, ringed Suillus usually found with pines. Some foragers use “slippery jack” loosely for other slimy-capped Suillus species, so compare the related Suillus entries before assuming a sticky Suillus is this species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Ringed slippery Suillus with a glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, and pine association. Treat the common name broadly only with caution: this page is for Suillus luteus.
mushroom_profile.ring: Ring present; whitish when young, often purple-brown beneath with age
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall; often with pine.
research_notes: Canonical Slippery Jack record; merged former suillus-luteus duplicate in v4.2.70.
Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.general_notes: Often abundant under pines. Texture is the limiting factor for many foragers; better young and firm than old and soggy.
records.review_note: Only one real photo currently loaded. Do not duplicate it to pad coverage; add more distinct photos later.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.
records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Slippery Jack record kept only as a legacy slug alias.
image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo batch-pack candidate appended in v4.2.62-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack2 (Batch B — Suillus and slippery-cap allies).
records.species_scope: In this guide, Slippery Jack means Suillus luteus specifically, not every slimy-capped Suillus.
records.look_alike_notes: Related Suillus species and loose “slippery jack” usage: these are comparison links, not a claim that every one is the same species.

use_links.notes: Ringed Suillus with glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, pine association, and Midwest notes.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended for meals in this guide; retained for identification and comparison with other yellow-pored boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Ashtray Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into early fall; local status needs review.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.

stem_feature: Reddish scabers or dots on yellowish stem

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Reddish-brown to wrinkled/slimy cap, yellow pore surface that does not bruise, yellowish stem with reddish scabers or dots, and yellow flesh that does not stain on exposure. Reported under hardwoods or conifers.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Hemileccinum rubropunctum
use_links.notes: Reddish scabers/dots, non-bruising yellow pores, and hardwood/conifer habitat.

records.edibility_notes: Use status and exact field limits need review. Do not put this in the pan just because it resembles mild yellow-pored boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Some Leccinum are eaten, but this group needs thorough cooking and careful host/tree notes. Do not treat scaber-stalk boletes as beginner mushrooms.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Birch Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Staining varies by species; slice and observe over 30 minutes
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
cap_surface: Dry cap; color varies by species
pore_color: Whitish to grayish or yellowish pores depending on species

records.edibility_detail: Leccinum species are host-specific and can be difficult to pin down in North America. Record host tree, staining changes over 30 minutes, pore color, and scaber color before using.
records.field_identification: Leccinum-type boletes have pore surfaces and a central stem covered with scabers rather than glandular dots or netting. Host tree is a key identification clue; many are birch, aspen, oak, or other specific-tree associates.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: The Genus Leccinum
use_links.notes: Scabers, host specificity, pore/staining notes, and North American ID caution.

records.notes: Blue-staining Slippery Jack: yellow-orange, finely velvety Suillus under two-needle pines, with pore/flesh staining blue.

mushroom_profile.summary: Blue-staining Slippery Jack: yellow-orange, finely velvety Suillus under two-needle pines, with pore/flesh staining blue. Commons-backed images added.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September with associated conifers or pines.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.

records.edibility_detail: A white-pine Suillus. Remove slimy skin if desired and cook thoroughly; avoid old soggy specimens.
records.field_identification: Grows with eastern white pine and other 5-needle pines. Bright yellow slimy cap often has red-brown fibers/scales; yellow pores are large and angular; stem has brownish glandular dots and may show veil remnants.
